data.title: Praxis surges after late-stage trial success for movement disorder therapy
data.description:
Praxis Precision Medicines (NASDAQ:PRAX) continued to climb on Thursday, adding more than 200% in the afternoon session as Wall Street welcomed a late-stage trial success for lixacaltamide, its experimental therapy for essential tremor, the commonest type of movement disorder.

Based on data from its Phase 3 Essential3 program, which comprises two simultaneously enrolled trials, Praxis (PRAX) said that the eight-week Study 1 hit the primary endpoint related to the change in a clinical measure known as mADL11.

According to topline data from Study 1, ulixacaltamide led to a statistically significant and clinically meaningful 4.3-point average improvement in the mADL11 score at Week 8 and achieved all secondary endpoints with statistical significance.

Study 2, which enrolled those who improved by 3 points in the mADL11, also hit the primary endpoint, as 55% of patients on ulixacaltamide maintained response compared to 33% in the placebo arm, with a statistically significant effect.

As for safety, Praxis (PRAX) noted that its experimental therapy was well-tolerated and added that there were no mortalities or treatment-related serious adverse events.

The company expects to submit a new drug application to the FDA in early 2026 to secure marketing approval for ulixacaltamide and request a pre-NDA meeting ahead of the filing.

Guggenheim and Jefferies were among the brokerages to boost their price targets on Praxis (PRAX) after the readout. With a Buy recommendation on PRAX, Guggenheim analyst Yatin Suneja lifted his price target to $350 from $155 per share, assuming an 80% probability of success for the drug’s approval.

Noting investor concerns regarding ulixacaltamide’s sales profile and high discontinuation rates, the analyst pointed to Essential3’s home-based trial design and argued that tolerability could improve in a commercial setup.

Meanwhile, with a Buy recommendation, Jefferies also raised its price target on PRAX to $300 from $65, increasing the drug’s probability of success for approval to 70% from 0% previously.

Update 1.00 PM ET: Adds latest stock move and comments from analysts

data.title: Vale nixes plan to build $231M Quebec nickel sulfate facility
data.description:
Vale (NYSE:VALE) said Thursday it has ended plans to build a C$325 million (US$231 million) nickel sulfate facility in Quebec after sole client General Motors (GM) pulled back its push to build electric vehicles due to weaker than expected demand and U.S. policy changes that make EVs less attractive.

GM (GM) will not immediately need nickel sulfate in Quebec because it has changed the timing of its Ultium Cam electric vehicle battery venture in Bécancour with South Korean steelmaker Posco (PKX), a Vale (NYSE:VALE) spokesperson told Mining.com, and "as a result, Vale Base Metals is cancelling its nickel sulphate plant project."

Vale’s (VALE) project would have been the first-of-its-kind fully domestic nickel sulfate facility for the North American market.

Vale (VALE), which has nickel mines in Ontario, Manitoba and Newfoundland and Labrador, as well as processing facilities, said it will continue to work with GM (GM) on their partnership to supply Canadian nickel into the automaker's North American supply chain.

data.title: Travelers anticipates $1.3B share repurchases in Q4 2025 while core ROE reaches 22.6% amid strong underwriting and investment income
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: The Travelers Companies, Inc. (TRV) Q3 2025

Management View

  • Alan Schnitzer, Chairman & CEO, opened with, "We are pleased to report excellent third quarter results. We have core income of $1.9 billion or $8.14 per diluted share. Core return on equity for the quarter was 22.6%, bringing our core return on equity for the trailing 12 months to 18.7%. Very strong underwriting results and higher investment income drove the bottom line."
  • Schnitzer highlighted, "Underwriting income of $1.4 billion pretax more than doubled compared to the prior year quarter, benefiting from both the lower level of catastrophe losses and higher underlying underwriting income."
  • Schnitzer noted net written premiums grew to $11.5 billion, with Business Insurance up 3% to $5.7 billion and Bond & Specialty Insurance at $1.1 billion. Personal Insurance had net written premiums of $4.7 billion. He emphasized, "With strong results over the past year in a particularly light cat quarter. We have a higher-than-usual level of excess capital and liquidity. Consequently, we anticipate a higher level of share repurchases over the next couple of quarters."
  • Daniel Frey, Executive VP & CFO, stated, "Core income for the quarter of $1.9 billion resulted in core return on equity of 22.6%, reflecting both excellent underwriting results and strong investment income. We generated higher levels of written premium and earned premium, while delivering excellent combined ratios on both a reported and underlying basis."
  • Frey added, "Catastrophe losses in the quarter were fairly benign at $402 million pretax consisting mainly of tornado hail events in the Central United States. Our outlook for fixed income NII, including earnings from short-term securities has increased from the outlook we provided a quarter ago, and we now expect approximately $810 million after tax in the fourth quarter."

Outlook

  • Frey provided guidance: "We continue to expect an expense ratio of around 28.5% for the full year 2025 and expect to manage to that level again in 2026. Our outlook for fixed income NII...has increased...to approximately $810 million after tax in the fourth quarter. For 2026, we expect more than $3.3 billion."
  • Frey also shared, "As a result, we expect to increase the level of share repurchases in the fourth quarter to roughly $1.3 billion. Also keep in mind that we previously shared our plan to deploy about $700 million from the sale of our Canadian operations expected to close in early 2026 for additional share repurchases as well."

Financial Results

  • The company reported core income of $1.9 billion and a core return on equity of 22.6%. Underwriting income reached $1.4 billion pretax, and after-tax net investment income was $850 million, up 15% year-over-year.
  • Net written premiums were $11.5 billion, with Business Insurance at $5.7 billion, Bond & Specialty Insurance at $1.1 billion, and Personal Insurance at $4.7 billion. The underlying combined ratio improved to 83.9%.
  • Operating cash flows for the quarter were $4.2 billion, and holding company liquidity ended at $2.8 billion. Adjusted book value per share was $150.55, up 8% from year-end and 15% from a year ago. Share repurchases for the quarter totaled $628 million, with $250 million in dividends distributed.
  • The company issued $1.25 billion of debt in July, maintaining a debt-to-capital ratio within its target range.

Q&A

  • Charles Peters, Raymond James: Asked about top line growth in 2026–2027 and technology/AI impact on expense ratio. Schnitzer replied, "We're not going to give outlook on the top line...We are very confident that we've got the right value proposition. We're investing in the right capabilities to make sure we're positioned to grow this business."
  • Peters also inquired about AI-driven savings. Schnitzer stated, "We are very bullish on AI, and we're leaning into it. We're spending more than $1.5 billion a year on technology. A lot of that is focused on AI."
  • David Motemaden, Evercore: Asked about renewal premium change ex-property and middle market premium growth deceleration. Toczydlowski explained the provided metric was for color and confirmed middle market quarterly growth was 7%.
  • Michael Zaremski, BMO: Asked about loss cost inflation and reserve release trends. Frey said, "We haven't explicitly commented on loss trend for a while...but it hasn't moved dramatically in recent period."
  • Tracy Dolin-Benguigui, Wolfe Research: Questioned capital deployment. Frey responded, "First objective for that excess capital is going to be to find a way to deploy it and generate a return...Once we've exhausted all those opportunities, then it's not our capital, it's the shareholders, and we're going to give it back through dividends and buybacks."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on growth prospects, expense reductions through AI, and premium retention, with some skepticism about top line momentum and the sustainability of positive trends, indicating a neutral to slightly positive tone.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, as reflected by Schnitzer: "We are very confident that we've got the right value proposition." There was a willingness to address uncertainty but with an emphasis on execution and strategic investments. Compared to the previous quarter, confidence remains strong, with management reiterating their strategic focus and financial strength.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance on share repurchases increased to $1.3 billion for Q4 from $628 million the previous quarter, and capital return intentions remain strong amid higher excess liquidity.
  • Net written premiums were flat at $11.5 billion quarter-over-quarter, though Business Insurance grew 3% in Q3 compared to 5% growth in Q2.
  • Core income rose from $1.5 billion in Q2 to $1.9 billion in Q3, while core ROE increased from 18.8% to 22.6%.
  • Underwriting income more than doubled compared to the prior year quarter, while catastrophe losses were lower at $402 million vs. $927 million in Q2.
  • Management's tone remained highly confident, with continued emphasis on technology, AI, and disciplined capital deployment. Analysts in both quarters pressed for more details on top line growth and expense management.

Risks and Concerns

  • Ongoing uncertainties in the economic, political, and geopolitical environment.
  • The loss environment, including weather volatility and social inflation on casualty lines, was highlighted as a continuing risk. Management reiterated their ability to confront these challenges with data, analytics, and disciplined reserve practices.
  • Analysts raised questions about premium growth deceleration, loss cost trends, and the impact of tariffs, with management noting only a small provision for tariffs in Q3 and indicating continued monitoring.

Final Takeaway

Travelers highlighted another quarter of strong profitability, with record core income, robust underwriting and investment results, and an improved core return on equity. The company plans to increase share repurchases to approximately $1.3 billion in Q4 2025, supported by elevated capital and liquidity, with additional buybacks anticipated following the closing of the Canadian operations sale in early 2026. Management expressed confidence in their value proposition, technology investments, and disciplined approach to growth and risk management, positioning the company for continued strong performance in the coming quarters.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Insteel expects $20M capex in 2026 while maintaining robust nonresidential demand outlook
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Insteel Industries (IIIN) Q4 2025

Management View

  • H.O. Waltz, President, CEO & Chairman, reported that the upturn in business activity continued during the fourth quarter, with fiscal 2025 acquisitions performing well. He noted, "we see no evidence of a broad-based slowdown in our markets, although housing continues to lag significantly as it has all year."
  • Waltz acknowledged ongoing recovery in markets but cited uncertainties from trade policies and the economic cycle.
  • Scot Jafroodi, VP, CFO & Treasurer, stated, "we delivered a strong fourth quarter performance, supported by higher shipment volumes and a continued recovery in spreads between selling prices and raw material costs." He reported net earnings of $14.6 million or $0.74 per diluted share, and highlighted a 9.8% year-over-year increase in quarterly shipments, driven by recent acquisitions and stronger demand in nonresidential construction.
  • Jafroodi noted that supply constraints for steel wire rod eased gradually, allowing improved production alignment and reduced lead times by quarter-end, but residential construction remained a headwind.
  • Waltz discussed the impact of Section 232 tariffs, stating, "the 232 tariff has caused domestic steel prices to rise to levels that reflect the 50% tariff on imports and predictably, imports have declined precipitously...making imports necessary for Insteel and other consumers."
  • Waltz confirmed successful integration of the Upper Sandusky, Ohio, and Texas acquisitions, reporting, "we consider the integration of these operations to be complete and successful."
  • Waltz outlined expectations for $20 million in capital expenditures in 2026, focusing on broadening product offerings, reducing cash production costs, and enhancing information systems.

Outlook

  • Jafroodi indicated, "Looking ahead to next year, we expect our effective rate will run around 23.5%, subject to the level of pretax earnings and other tax-related assumptions and estimates that compose our tax provision calculation."
  • Waltz expressed confidence in continued demand recovery, stating, "the demand recovery is nonetheless real...most customers and interactions between our salespeople and customers leads us to believe business conditions should remain reasonably robust into calendar 2026."
  • The company anticipates inventory to rise in the near term as additional import shipments are received, then normalize as raw material purchasing moderates.

Financial Results

  • Net earnings rose to $14.6 million or $0.74 per share, up from $4.7 million or $0.24 per share in the prior year period.
  • Quarterly shipments increased 9.8% year-over-year, but fell 5.8% sequentially from Q3.
  • Gross profit for the quarter was $28.6 million, with gross margin at 16.1%. Gross profit rose $16.3 million year-over-year, but fell $2.2 million sequentially from Q3, with gross margin narrowing by 100 basis points.
  • SG&A expense increased to $9.7 million, primarily due to higher compensation and $300,000 in amortization expense from acquisitions.
  • Cash flow from operations used $17 million in the quarter, compared to providing $16.2 million last year, largely due to a $37.4 million use of net working capital.
  • Inventory represented 3.5 months of shipments, up from 2.7 months at the end of Q3.
  • Capital expenditures for the year totaled $8.2 million, with $1.7 million incurred in Q4.
  • In fiscal 2025, $24 million was returned to shareholders through dividends and share repurchases, including a special cash dividend and 76,000 shares repurchased for $2.3 million.
  • The company ended the quarter with $38.6 million in cash and no debt.

Q&A

  • Julio Romero, Sidoti & Company: Asked if data center and infrastructure construction are still filling the gap from commercial and residential markets. H.O. Waltz responded, "I think it continues to be the fact that the data center construction is filling a hole that has existed in other markets...we think it will continue, but our lead times remain compressed just by the nature of the industry."
  • Romero asked about raw material supply status. Waltz stated, "we're not surprised at all by where we stand, and we're not disappointed by where we stand that we have what we need and the market -- the import market has changed some...so all things considered, we're exactly where we thought we would be."
  • Romero inquired about the contribution from Engineered Wire Products (EWP). Waltz explained, "the financial performance of Upper Sandusky has been solid and exactly where we thought it would be. It has a very attractive product mix."
  • Tyson Bauer, Kansas City Capital Associates: Questioned inventory carry strategy and margin variability. Waltz replied, "through our second quarter, inventories will be somewhat elevated relative to where they might be if we were acquiring raw materials domestically to a larger extent, but probably no higher than they are now."
  • Bauer asked about the sequential shipment volume decline. Waltz attributed it to production supply issues early in the quarter.
  • Romero asked about geographic demand strength. Waltz said, "I don't know that there are any geographic trends that jump out at us."
  • Romero inquired about water infrastructure spending in Texas. Waltz responded, "I think that part of the recovery in demand that we've seen has been related to the funding provided by the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act...I think those funds are beginning to find their way into the market and translate into demand."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral to slightly positive tone, focusing on demand trends, raw material supply, acquisition performance, and end-market diversification. Questions highlighted curiosity about sustainability of demand and the effects of tariffs and inventory strategy.
  • Management maintained a confident but cautious tone, with Waltz stating, "the demand recovery is nonetheless real...we believe business conditions should remain reasonably robust into calendar 2026." Management responses were generally detailed, though occasionally noted limited visibility into future trends.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's sentiment remained confident regarding nonresidential demand, but with increased caution around residential recovery and margin compression risks due to higher input costs. Analyst tone was similarly consistent, probing for clarity on strategic direction and operational impacts.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for capital expenditures increased from $11 million estimated in Q3 to $20 million for fiscal 2026.
  • Gross profit and margins narrowed sequentially, with gross profit falling $2.2 million and margin dropping 100 basis points, attributed to lower shipments and a slight decline in spreads.
  • Working capital usage increased, with inventory levels rising to 3.5 months of shipments versus 2.7 months at Q3-end.
  • Management continued to highlight nonresidential markets as the primary demand driver, while residential remained subdued.
  • The tone of management remained confident in prepared remarks but became more nuanced when addressing volatility in raw material costs and supply chain dynamics.
  • Analyst focus continued on the sustainability of demand, effects of tariffs, and integration of acquisitions, mirroring themes from the previous quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited persistent uncertainties due to trade policies and the economic cycle.
  • Risks from Section 232 tariffs were discussed, including their impact on costs and supply chain decisions.
  • Margin compression risk was noted for the first quarter of fiscal 2026 due to higher inventory costs.
  • Residential construction remains weak, with little expectation for a near-term recovery.
  • Macro indicators for construction remain mixed, and management noted monitoring of leading measures.

Final Takeaway

Insteel Industries enters fiscal 2026 with momentum fueled by strong nonresidential demand, successful acquisitions, and disciplined capital deployment. While the company highlights ongoing risks from tariffs and economic uncertainty, management believes the actions taken throughout 2025—particularly securing raw material supply and integrating new facilities—position Insteel to navigate near-term volatility and pursue long-term value creation. The company expects to invest $20 million in capital projects in 2026, further strengthening its operational foundation and market position.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Bny Mellon outlines 12% full year net interest income growth target while advancing commercial model and digital asset initiatives
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: The Bank of New York Mellon Corporation (BK) Q3 2025

Management View

  • Robin Vince, President, CEO & Chairman of the Board, highlighted another quarter of strong results, noting "Record revenue of $5.1 billion was up 9% year-over-year." He emphasized "double-digit revenue growth across the platforms that make up our Securities Services and Market and Wealth Services segments" and approximately 500 basis points of positive operating leverage. Vince reported a pretax margin of 36% and a return on tangible common equity of 26%, with earnings per share of $1.88, up 25% year-over-year.
  • Vince outlined product and client wins, including Franklin Templeton expanding its relationship with BNY to provide a full suite of asset servicing and FX capabilities for U.S.-listed ETF products, and TIAA selecting BNY's Wove platform as a unified wealth solution. He also described new digital asset initiatives such as BNY being appointed investment manager and primary custodian for OpenEden's Tokenized U.S. Treasury Bills Fund and a collaborative initiative with Goldman Sachs to create a blockchain-based mirror record of select money market funds.
  • Vince provided an update on the platform's operating model, stating more than 70% of employees are now working within the model, and projected full operational transition by fall of next year. He expects "the full benefits of these new operating rhythms until early 2028."
  • Vince announced a collaboration with Carnegie Mellon University to create the BNY AI lab, supporting research and deployment of AI. BNY launched Eliza 2.0, its next generation AI platform, and reported 117 AI solutions in production, a 75% increase compared to the prior quarter.
  • Dermot McDonogh, Senior Executive VP & CFO, stated, "Total revenue of $5.1 billion was up 9% year-over-year. Fee revenue was up 7%." He added, "Net interest income of $1.2 billion was up 18% year-over-year," and noted a benefit of $7 million in provision for credit losses. McDonogh highlighted a Tier 1 leverage ratio of 6.1% and CET1 ratio of 11.7%. He also reported a capital return of approximately $1.2 billion to common shareholders, resulting in a 92% total payout ratio year-to-date.

Outlook

  • McDonogh guided that "we expect net interest income in the fourth quarter to be approximately flat sequentially, which would ultimately result in full year 2025 net interest income to be up 12% year-over-year."
  • He stated, "We continue to expect expenses, excluding notable items for the full year to be up approximately 3% year-over-year."
  • McDonogh projected an effective tax rate for Q4 of approximately 21%, leading to a full year range of 21% to 22%. He expects capital return to remain consistent with "a total payout ratio of 90% to 100% for the full year 2025."

Financial Results

  • Securities Services reported total revenue of $2.5 billion, up 11% year-over-year. Segment pretax income was $806 million, up 26% year-over-year.
  • Market and Wealth Services segment delivered total revenue of $1.8 billion, up 14% year-over-year, with pretax income of $875 million, up 24% year-over-year and a pretax margin of 50%.
  • Investment and Wealth Management segment reported total revenue of $824 million, down 3% year-over-year, with pretax income of $184 million, up 5% year-over-year. Assets under management were reported as $2.1 trillion, flat year-over-year.
  • Firm-wide assets under custody/administration (AUC/A) of $57.8 trillion were up 11% year-over-year.

Q&A

  • Michael Mayo, Wells Fargo: Asked what portion of growth was due to company actions versus market conditions. McDonogh responded that "the intersection of the commercial model and the platform operating model... positioned the company in a much better way than previously to take advantage of the market opportunities when the markets are constructive" and that growth was due to "a mixture of organic growth, higher market levels and then FX."
  • Glenn Schorr, Evercore ISI: Inquired about derisking the balance sheet and interest rate risk management. McDonogh said, "The sophistication and the investment that we've made over the last 3 years in our tools and our risk management... gives us a real confidence around the baseline and the strength."
  • Brennan Hawken, BMO: Questioned the impact of capital markets activity on securities lending. McDonogh said there was nothing "noticeable" to highlight in securities lending changes, while Vince noted the business's value in adjacency to the collateral management franchise.
  • David Smith, Truist: Asked about money market fund headwinds and fee pressure. Vince described the developments as "opportunity" rather than turbulence and emphasized BNY's breadth across money market platforms.
  • Alexander Blostein, Goldman Sachs: Sought views on future operating leverage and margin potential. McDonogh indicated optimism, stating "we're still in early innings" and highlighted the impact of ongoing transformation and AI initiatives.
  • Ebrahim Poonawala, BofA: Asked if digital asset adoption is disruptive or accretive. Vince responded, "There’s certainly tangible revenue opportunity... but also eyes wide open about the fact that with change, you've got to get it right."
  • Betsy Graseck, Morgan Stanley: Probed on the sustainability of positive operating leverage. Vince noted, "We feel like we're still early innings, and we're just getting going."
  • Gerard Cassidy, RBC: Asked about cross-selling and product depth with clients. Vince said, "The number of clients who buy 3 or more of our services is up 40% over the past 2 years."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst questions reflected cautious optimism, with some pressing on the sustainability of growth and the impact of market conditions versus company-driven initiatives. Concerns about operating leverage, digital asset risks, and capital deployment were prominent.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone throughout the call, with Vince repeatedly expressing confidence in the strategy and runway for future growth. In the Q&A, management emphasized cautious optimism, discipline in capital returns, and readiness for digital asset evolution.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts maintained a similar level of cautious inquiry, while management's tone remained confident, underlining ongoing transformation and runway for growth.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue and net interest income growth remained robust, though EPS for Q3 ($1.88) was slightly lower than Q2 ($1.93), and return on tangible common equity decreased to 26% from 28%.
  • Securities Services and Market and Wealth Services saw continued double-digit revenue growth, while Investment and Wealth Management revenue declined slightly year-over-year.
  • Guidance for full year NII shifted from "high single-digit" (Q2) to 12% year-over-year (Q3), and expense guidance remained at approximately 3% growth.
  • Management's confidence level and forward-looking remarks remained consistent, with increasing emphasis on operational model transformation, AI, and digital asset initiatives.
  • Analysts continued to focus on sustainability of margins, growth sources, and capital management, consistent with the previous quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Vince acknowledged "uncertainty and multiple tail risks remain. Geopolitical conditions, trade policies, fiscal deficits around the world and the sustainability of enthusiastic markets, to name a few."
  • McDonogh noted the provision for credit losses was driven by "changes in the macroeconomic forecast, partially offset by higher reserves related to commercial real estate exposure."
  • Analysts raised concerns about the impact of stablecoin adoption, fee rate pressures in money markets, and the revenue implications of digital asset initiatives.
  • Management addressed these by emphasizing investment in digital asset infrastructure, balanced growth between trust and platform businesses, and disciplined capital deployment.

Final Takeaway

Management underscored that BNY Mellon's strategy is delivering tangible results, supported by record revenue, strong organic growth, and advancing digital and AI initiatives. The company remains focused on completing its operational transformation, expanding its commercial model, and capturing revenue opportunities amid evolving market dynamics, while maintaining disciplined expense growth and robust capital returns. The outlook for net interest income was raised to 12% growth year-over-year, and the company continues to leverage its diversified platforms and technological investments to drive long-term shareholder value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: XRP issuer Ripple buys treasury software firm GTreasury for $1B
data.description:
Ripple, the company behind the XRP (XRP-USD) and RLUSD (RLUSD-USD) tokens, is acquiring treasury management GTreasury for $1B, it said on Thursday, marking another move in its broader effort to expand into financial services via acquisitions.

GTreasury's enterprise treasury platform is used by Fortune 500 firms to handle cash, in addition to foreign exchange solutions and risk management. The deal will bring Ripple digital asset infrastructure to help customers move and manage liquidity in real-time.

Specifically, customers will be able to tap into the multitrillion-dollar repo market through prime broker Hidden Road, earning more on short-term assets. And cross-border payments will be enabled real-time, 24/7/365.

The combination of the two companies' capabilities means that "treasury and finance teams can finally put their trapped capital to work, process payments instantly, and open up new growth opportunities," Ripple CEO Brad Garlinghouse said in a statement. "For too long, money has been stuck in slow, outdated payments systems and infrastructure, causing unnecessary delays, high costs, and roadblocks to entering new markets -- problems that blockchain technologies are ideally suited to solve."

This marks Ripple's third major acquisition this year alone, it noted, after the purchases of Hidden Road and stablecoin platform Rail. 

The GTreasury deal is expected to close in the coming months.

data.title: Marsh outlines $400M savings target through Thrive program and rebranding as Marsh
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Marsh & McLennan Companies, Inc. (MMC) Q3 2025

Management View

  • John Doyle, President and CEO, stated the company had a solid third quarter, growing revenue 11% and underlying revenue 4%, as well as introducing significant strategic changes including a rebranding: "Earlier this week, we announced that we will change our brand in January from Marsh & McLennan to Marsh. Also in January, our stock ticker symbol on the New York Stock Exchange will change from MMC to MRSH."
  • Doyle highlighted the launch of Business and Client Services (BCS), unifying operations and technology teams under Paul Beswick, aiming for efficiency gains: "Our new brand strategy, the creation of BCS and the efficiencies we expect to gain are core parts of a new program we call Thrive."
  • The Thrive program will include automation and workforce actions, targeting $400 million in savings over three years, with $500 million in charges to achieve these efficiencies. "Over the next 3 years, we expect Thrive will generate approximately $400 million in savings with a portion being reinvested to drive additional growth. We will incur around $500 million in charges to achieve these savings."
  • Doyle underscored continued investment in AI and proprietary tools such as LenAI, Aida, and Sentrisk, with the intent to boost productivity and client value.
  • Mark McGivney, Senior VP & CFO, remarked, "Our third quarter results were solid, reflecting our strong position and execution despite a more challenging environment. Consolidated revenue increased 11% to $6.4 billion with underlying growth of 4%, which came despite a headwind from fiduciary interest income. Operating income was $1.2 billion, and adjusted operating income was $1.4 billion, up 13%."

Outlook

  • The company continues to expect mid-single-digit underlying revenue growth, solid growth in adjusted EPS, and its 18th consecutive year of reported margin expansion for 2025. Management stated, "For the full year, we continue to expect mid-single-digit underlying revenue growth, margin expansion and solid growth in adjusted EPS. Note that this outlook is based on conditions today, and the economic backdrop could be materially different than our assumptions."
  • Doyle commented on the insurance and reinsurance market: "We anticipate insurance and reinsurance market conditions seen so far this year will likely continue in 2026."
  • No comparison to analysts’ estimates can be made as estimates data was not provided.

Financial Results

  • Consolidated revenue for Q3 was $6.4 billion. Adjusted operating income rose to $1.4 billion, with an adjusted operating margin of 22.7%. GAAP EPS was $1.51, and adjusted EPS was $1.85, up 11% year over year.
  • Risk & Insurance Services (RIS) reported Q3 revenue of $3.9 billion, with Marsh revenue at $3.4 billion, reflecting integration contributions from McGriff. Guy Carpenter’s revenue was $398 million.
  • The Consulting segment posted Q3 revenue of $2.5 billion. Mercer’s assets under management reached $683 billion at quarter end.
  • Fiduciary interest income was $109 million for the quarter, with $85 million expected in Q4.
  • $400 million of stock was repurchased in the quarter.

Q&A

  • Charles Peters, Raymond James: Asked about growth sustainability amid a challenging pricing environment. Doyle responded, "We guided to mid-single-digit underlying revenue growth. I like how we're positioned; I am very excited about Thrive and what that can mean for our growth over time."
  • Peters followed up on the launch of a wholesale business in London. Doyle clarified, "We're not looking to build a third-party wholesale business here. We have exceptional specialty talent inside of this company... So it's a revenue synergy for us at McGriff, and we're excited about those possibilities in 2026."
  • Michael Zaremski, BMO, sought detail on the cost/savings ratio of the Thrive program. McGivney said, "We've got a pretty high degree of confidence in the savings and charges, although over time, these estimates might move a little bit."
  • Jamminder Bhullar, JPMorgan, asked about Oliver Wyman growth. Nicholas Studer, Vice Chair, explained, "Best quarterly growth in 6 quarters... we did benefit from some favorable timing on things like success fees."
  • David Motemaden, Evercore ISI, inquired about Thrive reinvestment. Doyle answered, "The investment will also be in accelerating our AI journey as well. We've continued to invest in talent, both organically and inorganically."
  • Robert Cox, Goldman Sachs, discussed international pricing sensitivity. Martin South, Vice Chair, said, "Our international growth... was 5% on top of 7% in 3Q '24 and underlying growth year-to-date is 6%."
  • Brian Meredith, UBS, asked about M&A appetite. Doyle stated, "Do we have the appetite and ability to do a larger scale deal? Absolutely."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism but pressed for clarity on growth sustainability, margin expansion, and Thrive program details, reflecting a slightly positive to neutral sentiment.
  • Management maintained a confident, upbeat tone in both prepared remarks and responses, using phrases like "we're confident in our ability to execute" and "we feel good about our growth."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone remained steady and confident, while analysts continued to focus on macroeconomic pressures and strategic execution.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The Q3 call featured new announcements on rebranding and the launch of the Thrive program, with a specified $400 million savings target and $500 million charges over three years, which were not present in Q2.
  • Q3 revenue growth slowed slightly versus Q2, while margin expansion and EPS growth continued in line with guidance.
  • The focus shifted from general macroeconomic and litigation environment in Q2 to operational efficiency, AI investment, and brand consolidation in Q3.
  • Analysts continued to probe on underlying growth, M&A, and operational execution, while management emphasized the potential of the Thrive program and AI to drive future value.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing macroeconomic uncertainty, declining P&C pricing, and economic hesitancy among U.S. clients as headwinds.
  • Doyle noted, "It feels like a pretty uneven economy to me... but we're not pessimistic about growth."
  • The company is exposed to declining fiduciary interest income and market competition, particularly in property and reinsurance rates.
  • Charges associated with Thrive and McGriff integration present near-term financial impacts.

Final Takeaway

Marsh & McLennan Companies is embarking on a significant transformation with a rebranding to Marsh, the rollout of the Thrive efficiency program targeting $400 million in savings, and continued investment in AI and talent. Leadership remains confident in sustaining mid-single-digit underlying revenue growth and margin expansion, even as the company navigates a complex macroeconomic environment and shifting industry dynamics.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Endeavour Silver achieves commercial production at Terronera mine
data.description:
Endeavour Silver (NYSE:EXK) said Thursday it has started commercial production at its Terronera silver and gold mine in Mexico, which is expected to process 360,000 metric tons of ore over the next six months with average grades estimated at 122 g/t silver and 2.52 g/t gold.

The news follows a successful commissioning phase in which Terronera's mining and processing operations were ramped up to consistently exceed an average of 90% of the designed nameplate capacity of 2,000 tons/day while also achieving at least 90% of projected metal recoveries.

Endeavour (NYSE:EXK) said Terronera has operated for 100 days since July 1, with eight days of downtime, and has sustained strong metal recoveries since mid-August.

The mine’s previous feasibility study forecast annual production of 4 million oz of silver and 38,000 oz of gold over a 10-year mine life.

Endeavour (EXK) shares +7.1% on Thursday to its highest level in 13 years, as precious metals prices rally to all-time highs.

data.title: Apple TV and Peacock introduce streaming bundle
data.description:
Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) and Comcast (NASDAQ:CMCSA)-owned NBCUniversal are launching the Apple TV and Peacock bundle starting at $14.99 per month from Oct. 20.

The companies said customers in the U.S. can save over 30% by subscribing to the Apple TV and Peacock Premium bundle for $14.99 per month or Apple TV and Peacock Premium Plus for $19.99 per month through either app or website.

Apple One subscribers on the Family and Premier plans can subscribe to Peacock Premium Plus and receive a 35% discount — the first benefit of its kind for Apple’s all-in-one subscription bundle, the companies added.

Under the collaboration, Apple TV app users and Peacock subscribers will be able to sample a selection of the partner services’ shows in each respective app.

At launch, Peacock subscribers can enjoy up to three episodes of Stick, Slow Horses, Silo, The Buccaneers, Foundation, Palm Royale, and Prehistoric Planet from Apple TV for free, while Apple TV app users will be able to watch up to three episodes of Law & Order, Bel-Air, Twisted Metal, Love Island Games, Happy’s Place, The Hunting Party, and Real Housewives of Miami from Peacock.

data.title: SA analyst upgrades/downgrades: MU, INTC, ASML, VALE
data.description:
Recent analyst activity highlights significant moves in both the upgrade and downgrade, with Intel’s (NASDAQ:INTC) strategic transformation earning it an upgrade, reflecting newfound confidence in its AI endeavors. Similarly, Vale (NYSE:VALE) receives a positive adjustment, indicating long-term value amidst fluctuating market conditions. In contrast, Micron (NASDAQ:MU) and ASML (NASDAQ:ASML) face downgrades due to concerns over future growth and valuation pressures.

Upgrades

  • Intel Corporation (NASDAQ:INTC): Upgrade from Neutral to Buy by Yiannis Zourmpanos. The analyst notes Intel’s significant investments in AI and strategic partnerships, positioning the company for future growth and revitalization.

    “Intel’s revival accelerates via $18 billion in new capital and historical partnerships with Nvidia, SoftBank, and the US administration. They fortify liquidity, endorse leadership, and connect Intel to AI and foundry ecosystems. Beyond near-term losses, tighter expenses and rollouts of AI chips set Intel up for sustained recovery and a long runway for multiple expansions.”

  • Vale S.A. (NYSE:VALE): Upgrade from Bearish to Neutral by Harrison Schwartz. Despite concerns about China’s iron demand, the analyst sees potential offset through India’s rising steel production and Vale’s profitable operations.

    “Vale’s immediate valuation is attractive at a ~6X forward “P/E.” Even if iron eventually crashes due to Chinese demand, its costs are low enough to remain profitable. Global iron supply investments are also low…so it may be very profitable if iron demand rebounds in the 2030s, as seems likely based on India’s economic development trajectory.”

Downgrades

  • Micron Technology, Inc. (NASDAQ:MU): Downgrade from Buy to Hold by Oakoff Investments. The firm’s recent rally is believed to have fully priced in potential upsides, with concerns about competition and future margins driving a more cautious outlook.

    “The stock has probably reflected all the fundamentally explained upside ahead, and I see that MU has already lost its previous undervaluation, even if its FWD multiples look attractive to some. The technicals suggest that MU may be primed for a corrective move to the nearest support zone.”

  • ASML Holding N.V. (NASDAQ:ASML): Downgrade from Buy to Hold by Jonathan Weber. The analyst warns of an overextended valuation amid modest growth forecasts, suggesting the stock may not warrant its current premium.

    “ASML Holding is a quality company with a strong market position, but paying more than 35x net profits while growth has slowed down does not seem like a great idea to me…At more than $1,000 per share and very close to the all-time high, I think ASML is too expensive for a ‘Buy’ rating right now.”
